front: Architect: pioneered the geodesic dome — a spherical structure that distributes stress over polyhedra with only triangular faces, embodying 'doing more with less'
Architect: designed the US Pavilion at Expo 67 on St. Helen's Island, Montreal; after a fire destroyed its acrylic membrane, the steel skeleton became the environmental museum 'Biosphere'
Architect: his geodesic style inspired Epcot's Spaceship Earth at Disney World (made of two domes); also inspired the Eden Project in Cornwall by Nicholas Grimshaw
Architect: designed circular steel shelters for WWII soldiers, inspired by the sight of metal grain silos
Architect: circular aluminum house suspended from a central mast with 'bicycle-wheel' spokes; featured an 'O-volving shelf' (revolving closet) and a 'fog gun' instead of a shower
Architect: Dymaxion House prototypes named 'Barwise' and 'Danbury' were built with Wichita's Beech Aircraft; the only surviving prototype was purchased in 2011 by the Henry Ford Museum
Architect: Dymaxion House had hexagonal walls (hard to hang pictures), translucent ceilings, and downdraft ventilation; shape compared to a Hershey's Kiss; descended from the '4D Lightful Tower'
Architect: Dymaxion House bathroom was made of two connected copper bubbles with strategically-placed drains; the 1952 Growth Home included a proto-form of a vertical indoor farm
Architect: coined 'Dymaxion' (portmanteau of dynamic, maximum, tension) as a prefix for his house, car, map, Chronofile, and sleep scheme
work: Dymaxion Map / Dymaxion Car / Dymaxion concept

front: Architect: Dymaxion Map projects Earth's surface onto an icosahedron that unfolds flat without directional distortion; beats Mercator for area accuracy; profiled in Life magazine
work: Dymaxion Map / Dymaxion Car / Dymaxion concept

front: Architect: Dymaxion Car was a three-wheeled 'zoomobile' co-designed with Isamu Noguchi; funded by Henry Kaiser; Noguchi drove it with Clare Boothe Luce; Alec Nevala-Lee documented its fatal accidents
work: Dymaxion Map / Dymaxion Car / Dymaxion concept

front: Architect: Jasper Johns created a painting of 22 triangular canvas panels named for the 'airocean world' and the Dymaxion map, inspired by Fuller
work: Dymaxion Map / Dymaxion Car / Dymaxion concept

front: Architect: Dymaxion Chronofile recorded his activities every 15 minutes for 63 years; 'Dymaxion sleep' consisted of half-hour naps once every six hours
work: Dymaxion Map / Dymaxion Car / Dymaxion concept

front: Architect: carbon-60 molecule 'buckminsterfullerene' (the buckyball) is named after him
Architect: coined 'Spaceship Earth'; his 1968 book was framed as its 'operating manual,' introducing the concept of 'Great Pirates'
Architect: coined 'tensegrity' (tensional integrity) — structural design where compressed components are isolated within a network of pre-tensioned cables
Architect: proposed Cloud Nine — floating spherical tensegrity habitats (with Shoji Sadao) that would stay airborne by maintaining internal temperature one degree higher than surrounding air
Architect: Synergetics design philosophy used 60-degree rather than 90-degree coordination; essay 'The Wellspring of Reality' decries specialism in favor of generalism
Architect: epitaph 'Call me Trim Tab' derives from a 1972 Playboy interview where he used the trim tab as a metaphor for individual empowerment
Architect: defined 'ephemeralization' as the growing efficiency of technology toward 'doing everything with nothing'
Architect: proposed Old Man River City — a new East St. Louis covered by a giant umbrella dome over a massive crater
Architect: Isamu Noguchi sculpted a chrome-plated portrait bust of him early in his career; they were lifelong friends who first met at a tavern suggested by Constantin Brancusi
Architect: second World President of Mensa; professor at Southern Illinois University; H.S.M. Coxeter's geometry was a major inspiration for his designs
Architect: student J. Baldwin, inventor of the Pillow Dome, was chief designer of the Whole Earth Catalog; Louis Kahn called his space frames 'hollow stones'
